name: Mundaka Upanishad
summary: The Mundaka Upanishad distinguishes between the lower knowledge (apara vidya — sciences, rituals) and the higher knowledge (para vidya — knowledge of Brahman). It teaches that Brahman is the source of all, like sparks from fire, and can only be known through a qualified teacher.

verses.sanskrit: द्वे विद्ये वेदितव्ये इति ह स्म यद्ब्रह्मविदो वदन्ति परा चैवापरा च ||१.१.४||
verses.transliteration: dve vidye veditavye iti ha sma yad brahmavido vadanti parā caivāparā ca ||1.1.4||
verses.english_translation: Two kinds of knowledge are to be known — so say the knowers of Brahman — the higher and the lower.
verses.shankara_bhashya_english: Shankara: The higher knowledge is the direct knowledge of Brahman. The lower knowledge comprises the four Vedas along with their six auxiliaries — phonetics, ritual, grammar, etymology, metre, and astronomy.

verses.sanskrit: तत्रापरा ऋग्वेदो यजुर्वेदः सामवेदोऽथर्ववेदः शिक्षा कल्पो व्याकरणं निरुक्तं छन्दो ज्योतिषमिति | अथ परा यया तदक्षरमधिगम्यते ||१.१.५||
verses.transliteration: tatrāparā ṛgvedo yajurvedaḥ sāmavedo'tharvavedaḥ śikṣā kalpo vyākaraṇaṃ niruktaṃ chando jyotiṣam iti | atha parā yayā tad akṣaram adhigamyate ||1.1.5||

word_meanings.meaning: by which the Imperishable is known

verses.english_translation: Of these, the lower is the Rigveda, Yajurveda, Samaveda, Atharvaveda, phonetics, ritual, grammar, etymology, metre, and astronomy. And the higher is that by which the Imperishable is known.
verses.shankara_bhashya_sanskrit: अपरा — चतुर्वेदाः षडङ्गानि च। परा — ब्रह्मज्ञानम् — यया अक्षरम् — अविनाशि ब्रह्म — अधिगम्यते।
verses.shankara_bhashya_english: Shankara: The lower knowledge comprises the four Vedas and their six auxiliaries. The higher knowledge is that by which the Imperishable Brahman is directly known. The lower leads to worldly and heavenly results; the higher leads to liberation.

verses.sanskrit:
यथा सुदीप्तात् पावकाद्विस्फुलिङ्गाः सहस्रशः प्रभवन्ते सरूपाः |
तथाक्षरात् विविधाः सोम्य भावाः प्रजायन्ते तत्र चैवापि यन्ति ||२.१.१||
verses.transliteration:
yathā sudīptāt pāvakād visphuliṅgāḥ sahasraśaḥ prabhavante sarūpāḥ |
tathākṣarāt vividhāḥ somya bhāvāḥ prajāyante tatra caivāpi yanti ||2.1.1||
verses.english_translation: As from a blazing fire thousands of sparks of the same nature leap forth, so from the Imperishable, O dear one, various beings arise and return there.
verses.shankara_bhashya_sanskrit: सुदीप्तात् पावकात् — प्रज्वलितात् अग्नेः — विस्फुलिङ्गाः — अग्निसरूपाः — प्रभवन्ते। तथा अक्षरात् ब्रह्मणः विविधाः भावाः — जीवाः — प्रजायन्ते।
verses.shankara_bhashya_english: Shankara: Just as sparks of the same nature as fire arise from a blazing fire, so from the Imperishable Brahman, various beings — individual souls — arise. And just as sparks return to the fire, all beings ultimately return to Brahman.

verses.sanskrit:
यथोर्णनाभिः सृजते गृह्णते च यथा पृथिव्यामोषधयः सम्भवन्ति |
यथा सतः पुरुषात्केशलोमानि तथाऽक्षरात्सम्भवतीह विश्वम् ||२.१.७||
verses.transliteration:
yathorṇanābhiḥ sṛjate gṛhṇate ca yathā pṛthivyām oṣadhayaḥ sambhavanti |
yathā sataḥ puruṣāt keśalomāni tathā'kṣarāt sambhavatīha viśvam ||2.1.7||
verses.english_translation: As a spider creates and withdraws, as plants grow from the earth, as hair grows from a living person — so from the Imperishable this universe arises.
verses.shankara_bhashya_sanskrit: ऊर्णनाभिः — मकडः — स्वतः तन्तुम् सृजति गृह्णाति च। तथा अक्षरात् ब्रह्मणः विश्वम् सम्भवति।
verses.shankara_bhashya_english: Shankara: Three analogies show that the universe arises from Brahman without any external material. The spider creates its web from itself; plants grow from earth; hair grows from the body. Similarly, the universe arises from Brahman alone, without any separate material cause.

verses.sanskrit:
तपति सूर्यो न तपति चन्द्रतारकं नेमा विद्युतो भान्ति कुतोऽयमग्निः |
तमेव भान्तमनुभाति सर्वं तस्य भासा सर्वमिदं विभाति ||२.२.१०||
verses.transliteration:
tapati sūryo na tapati candratārakaṃ nemā vidyuto bhānti kuto'yam agniḥ |
tam eva bhāntam anubhāti sarvaṃ tasya bhāsā sarvam idaṃ vibhāti ||2.2.10||

word_meanings.meaning: does not shine (by its own light)

verses.english_translation: The sun does not shine there, nor the moon and stars, nor these lightnings — how then this fire? Everything shines after that one shines. By its light all this is illumined.
verses.shankara_bhashya_sanskrit: तमेव भान्तम् — ब्रह्म एव प्रकाशमानम् — अनुभाति सर्वम् — सूर्यादयः अनुप्रकाशन्ते। तस्य भासा — ब्रह्मणः प्रकाशेन — सर्वम् विभाति।
verses.shankara_bhashya_english: Shankara: Brahman alone is self-luminous. The sun, moon, stars, and fire all shine by reflecting Brahman's light. Without Brahman's consciousness illumining them, no light could shine. This verse appears also in the Katha and Bhagavad Gita.

verses.sanskrit:
भिद्यते हृदयग्रन्थिश्छिद्यन्ते सर्वसंशयाः |
क्षीयन्ते चास्य कर्माणि तस्मिन्दृष्टे परावरे ||२.२.८||
verses.transliteration:
bhidyate hṛdayagranthiś chidyante sarvasaṃśayāḥ |
kṣīyante cāsya karmāṇi tasmin dṛṣṭe parāvare ||2.2.8||

word_meanings.meaning: the knot of the heart is cut
when the higher and lower are seen

verses.english_translation: The knot of the heart is cut, all doubts are severed, and all one's karmas are destroyed when the higher and lower are seen.
verses.shankara_bhashya_sanskrit: हृदयग्रन्थिः — अविद्याकामकर्मरूपः ग्रन्थिः — भिद्यते। सर्वसंशयाः — छिद्यन्ते। कर्माणि — क्षीयन्ते। परावरे — परम् च अवरम् च — ब्रह्म च जीवः च — दृष्टे — एकत्वेन ज्ञाते।
verses.shankara_bhashya_english: Shankara: The 'knot of the heart' is the knot of ignorance, desire, and karma. When Brahman is directly realized — when the higher (Brahman) and lower (individual self) are seen as one — this knot is cut, all doubts vanish, and all karmas are destroyed. This is liberation.

verses.sanskrit: ब्रह्मैव तेन गन्तव्यं ब्रह्म कर्म समुद्भवम् ||३.१.९||
verses.transliteration: brahmaiva tena gantavyaṃ brahma karma samudbhavam ||3.1.9||

word_meanings.meaning: Brahman alone is to be reached

verses.english_translation: Brahman alone is to be reached by one whose actions arise from Brahman.
verses.shankara_bhashya_sanskrit: ब्रह्मकर्मसमुद्भवः — यस्य कर्म ब्रह्मणः एव — स ब्रह्म एव गच्छति।
verses.shankara_bhashya_english: Shankara: One whose actions arise from the knowledge of Brahman — who acts without ego, as an instrument of Brahman — reaches Brahman alone. Action done in the spirit of Brahman leads back to Brahman.

verses.sanskrit: तमेवं विद्वानमृत इह भवति | नान्यः पन्था अयनाय विद्यते ||३.२.९||
verses.transliteration: tam evaṃ vidvān amṛta iha bhavati | nānyaḥ panthā ayanāya vidyate ||3.2.9||
verses.english_translation: One who knows this thus becomes immortal here itself. There is no other path for liberation.
verses.shankara_bhashya_sanskrit: एवम् विद्वान् — ब्रह्म एवम् जानाति — सः अमृतः इह भवति — जीवन्मुक्तः भवति। नान्यः पन्थाः — ब्रह्मज्ञानात् अन्यः मोक्षमार्गः नास्ति।
verses.shankara_bhashya_english: Shankara: One who knows Brahman as taught becomes immortal even while living (jivanmukta). There is no other path to liberation — not ritual, not austerity, not charity alone. Knowledge of Brahman is the only direct path to liberation.

verses.sanskrit: परीक्ष्य लोकान् कर्मचितान् ब्राह्मणो निर्वेदमायात् | नास्त्यकृतः कृतेन | तद्विज्ञानार्थं स गुरुमेवाभिगच्छेत् ||१.२.१२||
verses.transliteration: parīkṣya lokān karmacitān brāhmaṇo nirvedam āyāt | nāsty akṛtaḥ kṛtena | tad vijñānārthaṃ sa gurum evābhigacchet ||1.2.12||

word_meanings.meaning: having examined the worlds won by karma
the uncreated cannot be reached by the created

verses.english_translation: Having examined the worlds won by karma, let the brahmin come to dispassion. The uncreated cannot be reached by the created. To know That, let him approach a teacher.
verses.shankara_bhashya_sanskrit: कर्मचितान् लोकान् परीक्ष्य — कर्मफलभोगस्थानानि अनित्यानि इति विचार्य — निर्वेदम् आयात् — वैराग्यम् प्राप्नुयात्। गुरुम् अभिगच्छेत् — श्रोत्रियं ब्रह्मनिष्ठम् गुरुम् उपगच्छेत्।
verses.shankara_bhashya_english: Shankara: Having seen that all worlds won by karma are impermanent, the seeker should develop dispassion (vairagya). The uncreated Brahman cannot be reached by created means (karma). Therefore, one must approach a qualified teacher — one who is learned in scripture and established in Brahman.

verses.sanskrit: तद्विज्ञानार्थं स गुरुमेवाभिगच्छेत् समित्पाणिः श्रोत्रियं ब्रह्मनिष्ठम् ||१.२.१२||
verses.transliteration: tad vijñānārthaṃ sa gurum evābhigacchet samitpāṇiḥ śrotriyaṃ brahmanisṭham ||1.2.12||

word_meanings.meaning: with fuel in hand (as a student)

verses.english_translation: To know That, let him approach a teacher with fuel in hand — one who is learned in the Vedas and established in Brahman.
verses.shankara_bhashya_sanskrit: श्रोत्रियम् — वेदाध्यायिनम्। ब्रह्मनिष्ठम् — ब्रह्मणि निष्ठा यस्य। उभयगुणसम्पन्नः गुरुः।
verses.shankara_bhashya_english: Shankara: The qualified teacher must have two qualities: shrotiryam (mastery of Vedic scripture) and brahmanishtha (direct establishment in Brahman). A teacher with only scriptural knowledge but no realization, or realization without scriptural grounding, is insufficient.
